title: Saying Sorry
lesson: When you hurt someone, saying sorry helps make things better.
prompts: In a cosy sunny living room with a soft rug, warm afternoon light, the {INPUT_1_AGE} person from the input image stands playing happily with colourful wooden blocks, with a cheerful smile. Nearby, {GENDER_M_AGE_06} kneels beside a tall, neatly stacked block tower, looking proud. Make the interaction read physically: both children's attention is on the colourful wooden blocks between them — the child's hands on a loose block, the boy's hand resting on his tall tower. {INPUT_IMAGE_IDENTITY}

In a cosy sunny living room with a soft rug, warm afternoon light, the {INPUT_1_AGE} person from the input image is mid-step and reaching across, with a surprised expression as their arm bumps a tall block tower. Nearby, {GENDER_M_AGE_06} sits on the rug, both hands flying up in dismay, watching the colourful wooden blocks tumble and scatter across the floor. Make the interaction read physically: the child's arm strikes the tall block tower while the boy's raised hands and gaze follow those same blocks tumbling to the floor. {INPUT_IMAGE_IDENTITY}

In a cosy sunny living room with a soft rug, warm afternoon light, colourful wooden blocks scattered across the rug, the {INPUT_1_AGE} person from the input image stands, wringing their hands uncertainly in front of them, with a worried expression. Nearby, {GENDER_M_AGE_06} sits among the fallen blocks hugging his knees, with a downturned, sad mouth. Make the interaction read physically: the child's worried gaze turns toward the boy hugging his knees among the scattered blocks, and the boy looks down at those same fallen blocks. {INPUT_IMAGE_IDENTITY}

In a cosy sunny living room with a soft rug, warm afternoon light, colourful wooden blocks scattered across the rug, the {INPUT_1_AGE} person from the input image stands with one hand on their chest and the other hand gently open saying sorry with a sincere, apologetic expression toward {GENDER_M_AGE_06}, who sits among the fallen blocks, looking up and listening with a sad but softening expression. Make the interaction read physically: the apologising child faces the boy, and the boy looks up to meet the child's gaze. {INPUT_IMAGE_IDENTITY}

In a cosy sunny living room with a soft rug, warm afternoon light, the {INPUT_1_AGE} person from the input image is kneeling on the rug, picking up colourful wooden blocks and placing one block onto the rebuilt tower with a focused, kind smile. Nearby, {GENDER_M_AGE_06} kneels too, reaching toward that rebuilt tower with another block and beginning to smile. Make the interaction read physically: both children's hands add blocks to the one tower they are rebuilding, their gazes on that shared tower. {INPUT_IMAGE_IDENTITY}

In a cosy sunny living room with a soft rug, bright cheerful afternoon light, the colourful wooden block tower rebuilt tall behind them, the {INPUT_1_AGE} person from the input image shares a friendly hug, with a happy, warm smile. Nearby, {GENDER_M_AGE_06} hugs back, beaming with joy. Make the interaction read physically: the child and the boy wrap their arms around each other in one shared hug, the rebuilt tower standing behind them. {INPUT_IMAGE_IDENTITY}
gists: In a cosy sunny living room, the child plays happily with wooden blocks while a young boy kneels proudly by a tall block tower he built.

In the living room, the child, mid-step and reaching across with a surprised look, accidentally bumps the tall tower so the blocks tumble and scatter; the boy throws his hands up as he watches them fall.
In the living room with blocks scattered on the rug, the child wrings their hands, worried, while the sad boy sits among the fallen blocks — the child is unsure what to do.
In the living room with scattered blocks, the child stands alone with a hand on their chest and a thoughtful, sorry expression, hesitating to apologise.
In the living room, the child kneels helping rebuild the tower with an apologetic smile while the boy kneels too, starting to smile — the child says sorry and helps fix it.
In the living room with the tower rebuilt tall behind them, the child and the boy share a happy hug — saying sorry has mended the friendship.
texts: {NAME} was playing blocks with a friend. He had built a tall, wobbly tower. "Look how high it is!" he said proudly. "Wow!" said {NAME}.
As {NAME} reached across — crash! {NAME}'s arm bumped the tower and all the blocks tumbled down. "Oh no!" gasped {NAME}.
The friend looked at the fallen blocks and felt sad. "My tower..." he said quietly. {NAME} wasn't sure what to do.
{NAME} took a deep breath. It felt hard to say. "I know the right thing to do," {NAME} whispered bravely.
"I'm sorry I knocked it over," said {NAME}. "Can I help you build it again?" "Yes!" said the friend, starting to smile. Together they stacked it back up.
Soon they were laughing and hugging by the tall new tower. "Friends again!" said {NAME}. Saying sorry had made everything better.
